names.japanese_name: グルコノデルタラクトン
names.japanese_label_name: グルコノデルタラクトン

natural_occurrence.description: GDL is the lactone (cyclic ester) form of gluconic acid. It occurs in small amounts in nature and is produced commercially by purification and crystallization from gluconic acid solutions.

manufacturing.description: Produced by fermenting glucose to gluconic acid (using Aspergillus niger or bacteria), then crystallizing the lactone form. GDL forms naturally when gluconic acid is concentrated and cooled. The solid crystalline form is stable but slowly hydrolyzes back to gluconic acid in water.

other_uses.cosmetics: Exfoliant, pH adjuster, moisturizer (polyhydroxy acid, PHA).
other_uses.industrial: Textile treatment, concrete additives, metal cleaning.

eu.conditions: Permitted as acidity regulator, raising agent, and sequestrant in various food categories.

usa.notes: Widely used in tofu production and as leavening acid in baking.

japan.japanese_regulation_name: グルコノデルタラクトン
japan.notes: Widely used in tofu production in Japan.

safety_evaluations.efsa_adi: not specified (no safety concern at current use levels)

commonly_found_in: SILKEN TOFU (primary coagulant for smooth, delicate tofu)
Baking powder (slow-release acid for extended leavening)
fun_facts: THE secret to SILKEN TOFU - creates ultra-smooth, custard-like texture that nigari or calcium can't match
Acts as a 'slow-release' acid - dissolves and gradually releases acid over time
When mixed with water, it slowly converts back to gluconic acid, providing delayed acidification
This delayed action is perfect for tofu: allows soy milk to be poured and settled before coagulation begins
Used in cosmetics as a gentle exfoliant (polyhydroxy acid - milder than glycolic acid)
Also makes better-textured sausages by allowing proteins time to bind before pH drops
Popular in Chinese and Japanese tofu making for its ability to create smooth, uniform texture
